sound_type: caw caw caw complex -- the most recognized bird sound in North America, alarm caw, assembly caw, scolding caw, feeding call, rattling
ecosystem: Varied habitat. Cities, suburbs, farms, forests, beaches, parks, parking lots. The most habitat-generalist bird in the engine.
season: Year-round resident. Vocal throughout the year. Winter roosts largest.

coordinates.description: Reference for urban/suburban crow habitat in the PNW, Seattle metro area. Crows found in every PNW city and town.

taxonomy_note.description: THIRD CONSECUTIVE CORVIDAE (100-101-102). Corvidae trio. Same genus *Corvus* as Common Raven. New species: *C. brachyrhynchos*. Smaller, more social, more urban than the raven. Fourth Corvidae entry in engine (after Steller's Jay 53, Raven 100, Raven pair 101).
taxonomy_note.infraclass_note: Third consecutive Aves (100-101-102). Aves returned at degree 100.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Corvus from Latin corvus ('raven/crow'). brachyrhynchos from Greek brachys ('short') + rhynchos ('bill') -- short-billed, relative to the raven. Common name 'crow' from Old English crawe, imitative of the call.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Corvus -- same genus as Common Raven (*C. corax*). The American Crow is smaller, more social, more urban. Same lineage, different strategies.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Aves -- third consecutive (100-101-102). Birds continue after the centennial return.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Corvidae third consecutive (100-101-102). Unprecedented trio. Fourth total Corvidae entry (53, 100, 101, 102).

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Caw: 1000-4000 Hz. Higher-pitched than the raven's deep cronk (200-800 Hz). The caw has more energy in the upper harmonics, giving it a harsher, more piercing quality.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 1500-3000 Hz. The caw's peak energy is in the mid-high range -- carrying and attention-grabbing. Harsher and higher than the raven.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: High. Crows vocalize frequently throughout the day. Social calling is near-constant in groups. Dawn and dusk roost gatherings are particularly vocal.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual caw: 0.2-0.5 seconds. Caw sequences: 2-10 caws in rapid succession. Alarm sequences can continue for minutes.
song_metrics.note_structure: Simple caw repeated with variation in rate, intensity, and pitch. Less complex than raven but more recognizable. Different caw types carry different information.
song_metrics.note_types: Alarm caw (rapid, harsh, urgent). Assembly caw (rhythmic, moderate). Scolding caw (directed, persistent). Feeding call (softer, shorter). Rattling and clicking (close social). Juvenile begging call.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Repetitive caw sequences. Rate varies by context: rapid for alarm, moderate for assembly, persistent for scolding. Some call-response patterns between individuals.
song_metrics.timbre: Harsh, nasal, penetrating. The classic corvid rasp. Higher and thinner than the raven's deep, resonant cronk. Immediately recognizable.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Loud individually. Massive in groups. A roost of thousands is acoustically overwhelming. Individual crow not as powerful as raven pair (E=5).
song_metrics.tempo: Variable. Rapid alarm sequences. Moderate assembly calls. Persistent scolding. Social chatter can be continuous.
song_metrics.context: MULTI-FUNCTIONAL. Alarm (predator alert, mobbing coordination). Assembly (flock gathering, roost calling). Scolding (directed threat response, face-specific). Social coordination. Food discovery.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Some regional variation in caw structure documented. Individual recognition by voice within flocks. Cultural transmission of threat responses (face recognition spreads through populations).
song_metrics.key_property: CAW CAW CAW COMPLEX -- 102nd acoustic element (species side). The most recognized bird sound in North America. Alarm, assembly, scolding, feeding, and social caw types. E=4. CORVIDAE TRIO (100-101-102). Same genus *Corvus* as Raven. Most habitat-generalist bird. 42nd consecutive original CSV.

plumage.overall: Entirely black. Less iridescent than the raven. Smaller overall. When perched, the fan-shaped tail is diagnostic (vs raven's wedge).
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 102: (1) ENTIRELY BLACK but smaller than raven. (2) FAN-SHAPED TAIL -- diagnostic vs raven's wedge. (3) SHORTER, SLIMMER BILL -- not the raven's heavy bill. (4) HIGHER CAW -- not the raven's deep cronk. (5) SOCIAL -- usually in groups, not pairs.
plumage.flight_pattern: Constant flapping (vs raven's soaring). Direct flight. Often in groups. Does not soar on flat wings like the raven.

physical_description.overall: Medium-sized passerine. Length 40-53 cm. Wingspan 85-100 cm. Weight 0.3-0.6 kg. Entirely black. Fan-shaped tail. Slimmer bill than raven.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 102: (1) SMALLER than raven. (2) FAN TAIL in flight. (3) SLIMMER BILL. (4) HIGHER VOICE (caw not cronk). (5) FLAPPING FLIGHT (no soaring). (6) SOCIAL (groups not pairs).
physical_description.field_id: Medium-sized black bird, smaller than a raven. Fan-shaped tail in flight (raven = wedge). Slimmer bill. Flaps constantly (raven soars). Higher-pitched caw (raven cronks). Usually in groups (raven in pairs). If the black bird flaps, caws, and is with friends -- it is a crow.

nodes.title: Caw Caw Caw Complex -- 102nd Acoustic Element (species side)
nodes.description: The most recognized bird sound in North America. Alarm caw, assembly caw, scolding caw, feeding call, rattling. A language of urgency and social coordination. E=4.

nodes.title: Corvidae Trio -- Third Consecutive (100-101-102)
nodes.description: Unprecedented three consecutive Corvidae: Raven (individual), Raven pair (coordination), Crow (social). Three dimensions of corvid life.

nodes.title: Most Habitat-Generalist Bird in the Engine
nodes.description: Found everywhere: cities, suburbs, farms, forests, beaches, parking lots. Intelligence enables habitat generalism. More urban than any prior species.

nodes.title: The Campfire OK Contrast -- Both E=4, Both Familiar
nodes.description: Part A (Campfire OK, warm electronic, E=4) and Part B (Crow, familiar corvid, E=4). Both accessible, everyday versions of grander predecessors.

cross_references.relationship: SAME GENUS CORVUS, SIZE CONTRAST. Raven is larger, deeper-voiced, more solitary, open-forest. Crow is smaller, higher-voiced, more social, urban. Same genus, different strategies.
CORVIDAE TRIO (100-101-102). Three consecutive Corvidae. Individual (100), pair (101), social (102). Three dimensions of corvid life. Unprecedented.
FAMILIAR / UBIQUITOUS PARALLEL. Both most familiar, most recognized species. Mallard's quack and Crow's caw -- two most universally known animal sounds. Both habitat generalists.
FAMILIAR BIRD. Both most familiar North American birds. Robin's cheerful song and Crow's harsh caw -- complementary urban soundscapes. Both ubiquitous.
URBAN INTELLIGENCE. Both intelligent, urban-adapted, problem-solving. Raccoon manipulates with hands; Crow recognizes human faces. Both thrive in human landscapes.
